text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 04 October 2025 

Dr Amelia Carter  
South Thames Neurology Centre  
Department of Neurology, St Mary’s Way, London SE10 4AB  

Dear Dr Amelia Carter,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-03-1982, Hospital No: H937451 NHS No. 9038451127  
22 Brookfield Close, London, SE15 2PX 

Diagnoses:  

Recurrent unprovoked seizures with onset in adulthood (initial presentation 2015). MRI brain (2016) normal. Routine and sleep-deprived EEGs (2016, 2019) non-diagnostic.

Current Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily  
Vitamin D supplementation as per GP

Plan: 

Continue current antiepileptic regimen given durable seizure control and good tolerability  
Safety counselling reinforced; maintain regular sleep and avoid known precipitants  
Annual review via GP with open re-referral if breakthrough events, adverse effects, or pregnancy planning  
No routine neurology follow-up booked; patient has open access back to clinic via GP

I reviewed Michael Turner in my clinic today following referral for ongoing management of his seizure disorder. He reports durable seizure control, with no witnessed convulsive events or focal features since April 2021. He notes occasional brief moments of déja vu without loss of awareness occurring a few times per year, which have not progressed and do not impact function. There have been no injuries, emergency attendances, or medication lapses. Sleep has been regular and alcohol intake modest. He drives; I reminded him to comply with DVLA regulations and to inform the DVLA and insurer if any further events occur.

Levetiracetam 1,000 mg twice daily has been well tolerated without mood change, irritability, or cognitive complaints. Bloods from his GP in June 2025 (FBC, U&Es, LFTs, vitamin D) were within reference ranges. Prior MRI brain (2016) was normal, and both routine and sleep-deprived EEGs (2016, 2019) were non-diagnostic. Given the current durable seizure control and stability, I recommend continuing the present regimen. We discussed that any consideration of dose reduction would carry a risk of relapse; he prefers to continue unchanged at this time, which I support.

I have asked him to maintain sleep hygiene, avoid missed doses, and seek review promptly if there are any changes in event frequency, new neurological symptoms, mood changes, or treatment side effects. His GP will continue annual monitoring, and I am happy to review sooner should circumstances change.

With best wishes,

entities.text: no witnessed convulsive events or focal features since April 2021

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient has had no witnessed convulsive events or focal features since April 2021. Clinic date is 04 October 2025, so duration seizure-free = from April 2021 to October 2025 ≈ 4 years and 6 months (>6 months). Occasional brief déjà vu without loss of awareness occur a few times per year, but these are not clearly classified as seizures in the letter and are presented as non-progressive, not impacting function; the letter frames seizure control as durable. Given explicit phrasing of no convulsive events or focal features since April 2021, the patient meets seizure-free criterion for multiple years. Therefore normalize to 'seizure free for multiple years'.
seizure_frequency_number: no witnessed convulsive events or focal features since April 2021

flaws.description: Gold (seizure free for multiple year) disagrees with Reference (seizure free for multiple month)
